nativeRange.habitat: Sand or rubble patches on exposed outer reef flats, lagoon reefs, and seaward reefs; juveniles common in shallow tide pools, adults typically solitary on coral and rubble bottoms; generally reported from 2–30 m depth

decorPreferences: deep fine sand bed (10+ cm) for nightly burying and burrowing
extensive open swimming space along the tank length
robust live-rock structure capable of withstanding rock-flipping foraging
tightly sealed tank lid to prevent jumping
public-aquarium-scale footprint sized for an adult reaching ~1.2 m

diet.notes: In the wild has been documented as a significant reef predator feeding on hard-shelled invertebrates including sea urchins (which large adults have been observed bashing against rocks), crustaceans, gastropods, and other molluscs; trophic level is around 3.7. In captivity tends to accept a varied meaty diet — vitamin-enriched frozen mysis and brine shrimp, chopped marine fish and shellfish, and high-quality marine pellets and flake. Adults are voracious hunters and have been described as leaving 'no rock unturned' when foraging.

goodWith: similarly large and robust tankmates in public-aquarium-scale systems
avoidWith: any small or peaceful reef fish

summary: An Indo-Pacific wrasse that reaches roughly 1.2 m and develops a pronounced forehead bump; juveniles are colorful and frequently sold to home aquarists, but adult size and predatory behavior make it suitable for public aquaria only.
careNotes: The Twinspot Coris is one of the largest wrasses in the aquarium trade and is generally considered appropriate for public aquaria only. Juveniles are striking — white with bright orange patches and false eyespots on the dorsal fin — and are commonly imported at 5–10 cm, which masks the adult size; mature fish reach about 120 cm and develop a prominent gibbous forehead bump along with a uniformly dark green or dark blue body, often with pale midbody banding. Coris aygula is a protogynous hermaphrodite typical of Labridae: individuals start as females, with the dominant fish transitioning to male; only one male should be kept per system, and aquarists who want to attempt a group introduce multiple juveniles simultaneously. Adults are solitary in the wild and are best kept as single specimens in captivity. A deep, fine sand bed is generally considered essential — these fish sleep buried at night and dive into the sand when startled — and a tightly sealed lid is strongly advised, as wrasses of this genus have been reported to jump when spooked. The species is a voracious predator of hard-shelled invertebrates: ornamental shrimp, crabs, snails, urchins, and small molluscs will be eaten, and the fish has been observed flipping rocks and bashing urchins against the substrate to break them open. This makes the species incompatible with most reef and community systems, and it should be treated as not reef-safe once mature. Home aquarists who acquire juveniles should plan in advance to rehome the fish to a public institution before it outgrows the system.
breedingNotes: Captive breeding has not been documented in the aquarium hobby.

primary.name: LiveAquaria
primary.notes: Care profile retrieved at /products/twin-spot-wrasse after the originally-assigned /products/twinspot-coris URL returned 404. Lists 24 in (~61 cm) max, 300 US gal (~1136 L) minimum, 4–6 in sand layer, semi-aggressive temperament, reef with caution, moderate care level, 72–78 °F, pH 8.1–8.4, SG 1.020–1.025, dKH 8–12.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Cross-checked taxonomy (Labridae / Eupercaria), max length (120 cm TL — substantially larger than LiveAquaria's 61 cm figure; FishBase value adopted as biologically authoritative), depth range (2–30 m), temperature preference (24–28 °C — averaged with LiveAquaria 22.2–25.6 °C to a 23–27 °C working range), Indo-Pacific distribution (Red Sea and East Africa to Line and Ducie Islands, north to southern Japan, south to Lord Howe and Rapa), diet (hard-shelled invertebrates including urchins, trophic level 3.7), and reproductive mode (oviparous with distinct pairing). Sexual dimorphism noted: large males develop the gibbous forehead and an elongate first dorsal spine.

additional.name: Wikipedia — Coris aygula
additional.notes: Confirmed 120 cm maximum length, 2–30 m depth, gibbous forehead in mature fish, juvenile white-and-orange coloration with false eyespots on the dorsal fin transitioning to uniformly dark green or dark blue with light banding in adults, urchin-bashing predatory behavior, and IUCN Least Concern status.

fish.breedingNotes: Captive breeding has not been documented in the aquarium hobby. The species is oviparous and spawns with distinct pairing. As a protogynous hermaphrodite — a term for species where individuals begin life as females — the dominant fish in a social group will transition to male.

hobbyistAdvisory.reason: Reaches approximately 1.2 m as an adult, outgrowing any reasonable home aquarium. Adult fish are voracious predators of ornamental invertebrates (urchins, shrimp, crabs, molluscs) and develop a prominent forehead bump that signals public-aquarium-scale husbandry. Juveniles imported into the hobby need a planned rehoming pathway to a public institution before maturity.
